text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic appendicitomy. This patient has acute appendicitis. She is a female and ultrasound and CT scan both has Confirm the edematous inflamed appendix. Total count was also raised and she has a severe pain in right iliac fossa together with the rebound tenderness, Robson's sign. So it is a confirmed case of acute appendicitis.
The surgeon introduces a Veress needle into the inferior crease of the umbilicus, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm proper placement.

The surgeon attaches the tubing of the insufflator to initiate pneumoperitoneum, with the preset pressure set to fifteen. The actual pressure gradually increases, starting with a flow rate of one liter per minute, then increasing to three liters per minute. Once pneumoperitoneum is achieved, the incision is enlarged to eleven millimeters.

A smiling incision is made over the crease of the umbilicus, and the first optical trocar is introduced with a hissing sound. The telescope reveals the appendix, showing a healthy base but an inflamed and adhered tip. The appendix is retrocecal and subserous, adhered to the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate the inflamed and adhered appendix tip from the small intestine and surrounding structures. The appendix appears retrocecal and subserous, with visible adhesions requiring careful dissection.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum covering part of the appendix using a Striker mini alligator instrument to examine the base of the appendix. Additional peritoneal dissection is performed to mobilize the appendix, revealing the inflamed tip adhered to the cecum. The surgeon prepares to use an extracorporeal knot to secure the structure, starting from the base to avoid perforation while separating the tip.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the appendix and uses a harmonic scalpel with a Teflon jaw to carefully create a small window at the base of the appendix. The dissection is performed from both sides to avoid perforating the cecum or appendix.

The surgeon completes the blunt dissection through the buttonhole at the base of the appendix, using a Vicryl number one suture to create an extracorporeal Misra's knot. The knot is formed with three winds and locks, and is pushed to ligate the base of the appendix securely using a knot pusher.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the appendix, leaving a five-millimeter margin towards the cecum. The energy from the harmonic seals the distal end, preventing fecolith slippage. The base of the appendix is separated and ligated, and the surgeon carefully dissects the appendix from the subserous area of the cecum, progressing towards the inflamed and adhered tip.

With one end of the appendix free, the surgeon uses a grasper to apply anterior traction while employing a combination of blunt dissection, sharp dissection, and energy devices to carefully dissect towards the tip of the appendix. Minor oozing is observed but not immediately addressed to avoid injury to the cecum.

The surgeon carefully maintains a safe distance from the cecum while using a stripping technique to separate the inflamed and adhered appendix. Coagulation is applied, and the appendix is stripped down towards the tip. The tip of the appendix is adhered, and the surrounding fat is resistant to dissection. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to sacrifice part of the tip, cutting it directly to avoid perforating the ascending colon or cecum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate the appendix from the ascending colon and cecum, ensuring the Teflon jaw is positioned towards the cecum. The entire appendix is then separated, and the base is held with a cloth forceps to pull the appendix into the cannula.

The surgeon uses an endo bag to contain the appendix and removes it through the cannula. Betadine irrigation is applied and suctioned out to avoid irritation. The patient is tilted to the right side, and the ascending colon and cecum are submerged in saline. The surgeon presses on these structures to check for bubbles, indicating potential perforation.

The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the surgical area, ensuring all injected betadine is removed. The absence of bubbles during the flat tire test confirms the cecum is intact. No drain is required for this case of acute appendicitis with a retrocecal appendix.
